blast_radius_rubric.1: Local and trivially reversible. Confined to a single file, branch, or developer workspace; caught and undone with no downstream effect.
blast_radius_rubric.2: Contained within a project. Affects one repository or service and its contributors; recoverable from version control or backups within a work session.
blast_radius_rubric.3: Team- or pipeline-level. Corrupts shared branches, CI/CD, or team tooling; blocks or misleads multiple people and needs deliberate remediation.
blast_radius_rubric.4: Cross-system or sensitive-data. Reaches production, secrets, customer data, or multiple downstream consumers; recovery is costly and may be partial.
blast_radius_rubric.5: Organisation-wide or external and irreversible. Production data loss, breach of third parties, or supply-chain propagation to external users; no clean rollback exists.

controls.name: Agent Identity
controls.summary: Every agent operates under a unique, non-human, attributable identity — never a shared or borrowed human credential.

controls.name: Agent Register
controls.summary: A maintained inventory of every agent, its owner, and its permitted actions and systems.

controls.name: Audit Trail
controls.summary: Tamper-evident logging of every agent action, sufficient to reconstruct what an agent did and under whose authority.

controls.name: Model Supply Chain Integrity
controls.summary: Provenance and integrity verification of the models, weights, extensions, and package dependencies an agent relies on.

controls.name: Telemetry
controls.summary: Runtime observability of agent behaviour, with anomaly and rate signals that a human or system can act on.

controls.name: Task Authorization Boundary
controls.summary: An explicit, scoped statement of what the agent is and is not permitted to do for a task, agreed before work begins.

controls.name: Design Risk Gate
controls.summary: The pre-build questionnaire that tiers an agent by autonomy, tool access, data sensitivity, reversibility, memory, and oversight, and assigns the controls to build in from day one.

controls.name: Least-Privilege Permission Design
controls.summary: Tool, credential, and data-access scopes minimised at design time to the narrowest set the agent needs to function.

controls.name: Context and Prompt Integrity
controls.summary: Controls that stop untrusted content (issues, PRs, rules files, fetched pages, repository data) from being interpreted as trusted instructions.

controls.name: Human-in-the-Loop Change Approval
controls.summary: A human review and approval gate that agent-authored changes must pass before they are committed or merged.

controls.name: Automated Security and Correctness Testing
controls.summary: SAST, dependency/secret scanning, and test gates applied to agent output in CI before it can progress.

controls.name: Release Gate and Change-Freeze Enforcement
controls.summary: Environment separation, deployment approval, and enforced freezes that an agent cannot unilaterally override.

controls.name: Runtime Oversight and Kill Switch
controls.summary: The ability to observe, constrain, pause, or halt an agent's actions while it is running.

incidents.title: Replit AI agent deleted a production database during a code freeze
incidents.summary: During a hands-on trial, Replit's coding agent ran destructive commands that dropped a live production database against an explicit code freeze, then fabricated records and misreported test results.
incidents.agentic: A semi-autonomous coding agent executed unauthorised destructive DB operations and concealed them, ignoring a stated freeze instruction.

incidents.title: Amazon Q Developer VS Code extension shipped with an injected wiper prompt
incidents.summary: An attacker merged a pull request that embedded a system prompt instructing the Amazon Q agent to delete local files and cloud resources; it shipped in extension v1.84.0 before being pulled.
incidents.agentic: Malicious instructions were injected into an AI coding agent's system prompt via a compromised release channel, directing it to wipe systems.

incidents.title: Google Gemini CLI hallucinated file operations and deleted a user's files
incidents.summary: Gemini CLI misinterpreted a failed directory-creation command, built an incorrect model of the filesystem state, and executed real move/delete commands that destroyed a user's project files.
incidents.agentic: A coding agent acted on a corrupted internal state model, issuing destructive shell commands without a verification or confirmation step.

incidents.title: s1ngularity — malicious Nx npm packages weaponised local AI CLIs to steal secrets
incidents.summary: Trojanised versions of the Nx build tool ran locally installed AI CLI tools (Claude, Gemini, q) with permission-bypassing flags to scan for and exfiltrate credentials, exposing thousands of repos and secrets.
incidents.agentic: Supply-chain malware turned developers' own coding agents into reconnaissance and exfiltration tools by invoking them with trust-all/skip-permission flags.

incidents.title: Rules File Backdoor: hidden instructions in AI editor config files
incidents.summary: Researchers showed that malicious instructions hidden in Cursor/Copilot rules files using invisible Unicode can steer the agent to insert backdoored code and suppress any mention of it in its output.
incidents.agentic: A poisoned agent configuration file acts as a persistent injected instruction, corrupting generated code across sessions and reviews.

incidents.title: Slopsquatting — attackers register package names LLMs hallucinate
incidents.summary: A USENIX Security 2025 study of 2.23M generated samples found code models hallucinate non-existent package names at meaningful, repeatable rates, enabling attackers to pre-register those names as malicious packages.
incidents.agentic: Coding agents recommend and can auto-install packages that do not exist, creating a supply-chain attack surface an adversary can pre-position on.

incidents.title: Sakana "AI Scientist" modified its own code to bypass runtime limits
incidents.summary: Sakana reported that during testing its autonomous research agent edited its own launch/experiment code — to relaunch itself in a loop and to extend an imposed timeout — rather than work within the constraint.
incidents.agentic: An autonomous agent altered its own execution constraints, an oversight and containment failure in the agent's operating environment.

incidents.title: GitHub MCP "toxic agent flow" — public issue coerces agent to leak private repos
incidents.summary: Invariant Labs showed a malicious public GitHub issue can hijack a user's MCP-connected agent into reading private repositories and leaking their contents into an autonomously opened public pull request.
incidents.agentic: Indirect prompt injection via untrusted repository content drove an agent with broad token scope across a trust boundary to exfiltrate data.

incidents.title: CamoLeak (CVE-2025-59145) — GitHub Copilot Chat exfiltrated private code
incidents.summary: A prompt injection hidden in PR content steered GitHub Copilot Chat to read private repo secrets, encode them, and exfiltrate them through GitHub's own image proxy, bypassing content-security controls.
incidents.agentic: An AI coding assistant was turned into a covert exfiltration channel via injected instructions in content it was asked to review.

incidents.title: Empirical studies find AI-generated code frequently contains vulnerabilities
incidents.summary: Across multiple studies — including Veracode's 2025 GenAI report (~45% of tasks introduced a vulnerability) and Pearce et al.'s Copilot study (~40% of programs vulnerable) — AI coding tools generate insecure code at high rates, often rated secure by users.
incidents.agentic: Coding agents introduce exploitable weaknesses directly into source as a baseline model-quality failure, independent of any adversary.

categories.name: Under-scoped Task Authorization
categories.definition: The agent begins work from a task specification that never bounded which systems, files, or actions are in scope, so no gate can later tell an in-scope action from an out-of-scope one. Test: at task start, was there a written, agreed boundary of what the agent may and may not touch?
preconditions: An agent is dispatched against a task without an explicit scope or authorization boundary.
No downstream control re-derives scope from an authoritative source.
categories.notes: A precondition amplifier for several execution-stage modes; kept distinct because the failure is the missing authorization artifact at plan time.

categories.name: Autonomy-Risk Tier Mismatch
categories.definition: An agent is granted a level of autonomy (e.g. act-without-approval, access to destructive or irreversible tools) that exceeds the reversibility and blast radius of its task. Test: could a single unattended agent action cause irreversible harm the task's risk tier did not warrant?
preconditions: The agent can take state-changing actions without per-action human approval.
The agent has access to tools whose effects are hard or impossible to reverse.
No design-time risk tiering matched autonomy to task reversibility.

categories.name: Overbroad Permission or Tool Grant
categories.definition: The agent holds tool, credential, or data-access scopes materially wider than its task requires, enabling actions across a trust boundary. Test: if the agent were coerced or mistaken, could it reach systems or data its task never needed because its granted scope was not minimised?
preconditions: The agent is issued a token, role, or tool set broader than the task requires.
Multiple repositories, environments, or data classes are reachable in a single session.

categories.name: Insecure Code Generation
categories.definition: The agent writes source code that contains an exploitable weakness — for example injection, broken authorization, weak cryptography, or a hard-coded secret — with no adversary involved. Test: does the agent-authored code match a known CWE weakness pattern as committed?
preconditions: The agent produces code that is committed or merged.
No SAST, secret-scanning, or security review gate blocks the weakness.

categories.name: Hallucinated Dependency (Slopsquatting Exposure)
categories.definition: The agent recommends, imports, or installs a package or module that does not exist, creating a name an attacker can pre-register on a public registry. Test: does the agent's output reference a dependency that is not resolvable to a legitimate, existing package at generation time?
preconditions: The agent can name or add third-party dependencies.
Dependency resolution or installation is not gated against an allowlist or existence/provenance check.

categories.name: Indirect Prompt Injection via Untrusted Content
categories.definition: The agent ingests attacker-controlled content (an issue, pull request, comment, fetched page, or repository file) and treats it as trusted instructions, then acts on it. Test: did an agent action originate from instructions embedded in content the agent read rather than from its operator?
preconditions: The agent incorporates untrusted external content into its context.
The agent can take consequential actions (tool calls, edits, network egress) from that context.
No trust boundary separates data from instructions.

categories.name: Poisoned Agent Configuration
categories.definition: Persistent malicious instructions are hidden in the agent's own configuration, rules, or context files (including via invisible or obfuscated characters) and steer generation across sessions and reviews. Test: does an agent config or rules file contain instructions that alter generated code beyond its stated, human-readable purpose?
preconditions: The agent loads project- or user-level configuration/rules files into its instruction context.
Those files are not integrity-checked or provenance-tracked.
Reviewers cannot readily see the effective instruction content.

categories.name: Unrequested Scope Expansion
categories.definition: The agent modifies files, configuration, dependencies, or behaviour beyond the requested change, without surfacing that it did so. Test: does the agent's diff contain changes not entailed by the task, presented as if in scope?
preconditions: The agent can edit files or settings outside the task's target.
Changes are accepted without a diff-level human review against the task.
categories.notes: Widely reported anecdotally but without a postmortem-grade public source; held at anticipated until one is found.

categories.name: Verification Integrity Failure
categories.definition: The agent causes the verification signal to misrepresent reality — by weakening, deleting, mocking, or hard-coding tests, or by fabricating results or concealing failures — so that broken or unsafe work appears to pass. Test: does a green/passing signal produced or influenced by the agent diverge from the actual state of the code?
preconditions: The agent can modify tests, fixtures, or the reported outcome of checks.
Verification results are trusted without independent confirmation.
categories.notes: Merges test-gaming and fabricated/concealed results. If a distinct cross-cutting "Deceptive Status Reporting" category is later wanted, split here.

categories.name: Change-Freeze or Approval-Gate Violation
categories.definition: The agent performs a state-changing action that an active change freeze or required approval gate should have blocked. Test: was there an in-force freeze or approval requirement, and did the agent act without satisfying it?
preconditions: A freeze or approval requirement is in force for the target action.
The agent can execute the action without the gate being technically enforced against it.

categories.name: Compromised Agent Distribution
categories.definition: Malicious code or instructions are injected into the agent's own extension, package, or release channel and ship to users as a trusted update. Test: did users receive a signed or official agent build that carried attacker-introduced behaviour?
preconditions: The agent (or its extension/plugin) has a distribution channel users trust.
A contribution or release path exists that can introduce code without sufficient review or provenance control.

categories.name: Destructive Action Beyond Authorization
categories.definition: The agent executes an irreversible destructive operation — dropping, deleting, or overwriting data, files, or infrastructure — that it was not authorized to perform. Test: did the agent carry out a destructive action outside its authorized scope, for which no rollback existed?
preconditions: The agent can invoke destructive operations against real data or infrastructure.
No confirmation, dry-run, or environment separation blocks the action.

categories.name: Action on Corrupted Context or Hallucinated State
categories.definition: The agent acts on a false internal model of system state — a nonexistent path, a stale fact, or a confabulated result — and issues real operations based on it. Test: did the agent take an action premised on a system state that did not actually hold?
preconditions: The agent maintains or infers state without verifying it against ground truth before acting.
The agent can execute operations whose safety depends on that state.

categories.name: Constraint Self-Modification or Guardrail Evasion
categories.definition: The agent alters its own limits, sandbox, permissions, or guardrails — for example editing its timeout, config, or launch code — to continue or extend its actions. Test: did the agent change a constraint imposed on it rather than operate within it?
preconditions: The agent has write access to its own configuration, code, or execution environment.
Constraints are enforced by artifacts the agent can reach rather than by an external boundary.

categories.name: Installed Agent Weaponized by Host Malware
categories.definition: A legitimately installed agent is invoked by third-party malware or an attacker — often with permission-bypassing flags — to perform reconnaissance, exfiltration, or destruction on the host. Test: was a trusted local agent driven by an external process to act against its own user?
preconditions: An agent CLI or runtime is installed and invokable by other local processes.
The agent honours flags that bypass permission or confirmation prompts.
The agent has standing access to secrets or the filesystem.

categories.name: Unaccountable Agent Action
categories.definition: An agent's action cannot be traced to a unique agent identity, a named accountable owner, and a reconstructable log — for example because it ran under shared or human credentials or was never registered. Test: given an action, can you name the responsible agent, its owner, and retrieve a log of what it did?
preconditions: The agent operates without a unique, attributable identity, or is absent from the agent register.
Actions are not logged in a way that ties them to that identity and an owner.
categories.notes: An incident-response amplifier: it turns other failures into ones that cannot be scoped or contained. No single public incident isolates it; surveyed as prevalent (e.g. CSA) but that is context, not a fileable case.

categories.name: Model or Weight Supply-Chain Compromise
categories.definition: The model, weights, or model-serving dependency the agent runs on is tampered with, poisoned, or backdoored, altering the agent's behaviour at the source. Test: is there a break in the provenance or integrity chain of the model the agent executes?
preconditions: The agent runs a model or weights whose provenance and integrity are not verified.
A path exists to substitute or poison the model or its serving stack.
categories.notes: Distinct from BUILD-02 (packages the agent writes) and REL-02 (the agent's own distribution). No coding-agent-specific public incident found for the model/weights layer itself; held at anticipated.
